target.title: feat(hooks): emit session prune lifecycle event
target.body:
## Why Context pruning changes model input quality and cost, but there was no dedicated internal lifecycle signal for pruning actions. This PR emits a pruning lifecycle hook with safe summary metadata so pruning behavior is observable without exposing pruned content. Closes #8606 ## Detailed Changes - Added internal lifecycle event: - `session:prune` - Pruning hook context includes summary metadata: - `softTrimmedCount` - `hardClearedCount` - `toolNames` - `sessionId` (when available) - Updated context-pruning extension/pruner tests for async path + hook emission behavior ## Related Links and Issues - Existing source PR: #9761 - Closes #8606

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a new internal hook lifecycle event, `session:prune`, emitted by the context pruning path. The pruner now tracks how many tool results were soft-trimmed vs hard-cleared (and which tool names were affected), and passes this safe summary metadata into an internal hook event. The context-pruning extension’s `context` handler was updated to be async to await pruning, and tests were updated to cover the async path and hook emission behavior. Within the codebase, this integrates into the existing internal hook system (`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`) by creating and triggering an `InternalHookEvent` of type `session` and action `prune`, keyed by a derived session key.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is mostly safe to merge, but there is one correctness issue around the emitted hook event timestamp semantics that should be clarified/fixed. - The changes are localized (pruner + extension + tests + docs) and align with existing internal hook infrastructure. The main concern is that the emitted hook event uses a timestamp created at emission time, which can be meaningfully later than the prune operation and may mislead consumers relying on timing semantics. - src/agents/pi-extensions/context-pruning/pruner.ts

target.title: #93250: add download/wait-for-download actions to browser tool
target.body:
## Summary

Add `download` and `wait-for-download` actions to the browser tool so agents are notified when a click triggers a file download. Currently, clicking a download link saves the file to /tmp/openclaw/downloads but the agent has no way to know the download completed — it keeps retrying.

## Root Cause

The server-side download infrastructure already existed and was fully functional. However, the agent tool schema and runtime routing never exposed these actions — `"download"` and `"wait-for-download"` were missing from `BROWSER_TOOL_ACTIONS`, no client API functions were registered, and the tool dispatch logic had no `case "download"` or `case "wait-for-download"` branches.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Expose two new browser tool actions (`download` and `wait-for-download`) by adding them to the schema, client API, runtime barrel, tool dispatch, and test suite. Also addresses 3 ClawSweeper P2 issues from initial review: transport timeout derivation, type narrowing in tests, and lint cleanup.

**Real setup tested**:
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js v22.22.1
- Setup: openclaw development workspace

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
1. Validate schema accepts `"download"` and `"wait-for-download"` as valid actions
2. Verify tool description mentions download
3. Confirm `download` without `ref` throws
4. Run the full regression test suite (74 tests)

**After-fix evidence**:

```
=== Issue #93250: Browser Download Tool Action ===
--- Schema: accepted actions ---
  ✓ action="download" is valid
  ✓ action="wait-for-download" is valid
--- Tool description ---
  ✓ tool description mentions download
--- Download action validation ---
  ✓ download without ref throws: "ref required"
--- wait-for-download action ---
  ✓ wait-for-download without path succeeds (optional)
=== Summary ===
- Added action: download (click ref + wait for download, returns path)
- Added action: wait-for-download (wait for pending download)
- Added schema param: path (download destination path)
- Server routes POST /download and POST /wait/download already existed
- Tests: 5 new tests (74 total) all pass

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  74 passed (74)
```

**P2 issues addressed in v2 commit**:
- Transport timeout: derives fetch timeout from `opts.timeoutMs + 5s` buffer (fallback 120s) instead of hardcoded 60000ms
- Type narrowing: stores `result?.content[0]` in local variable and narrows `ImageContent | TextContent` union before reading `.text`
- Lint cleanup: removes unused `result` variable, types catch callback via `instanceof Error` with `String(err)` fallback

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- --run extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` passes (74 tests)
- [x] Change is minimal and targeted to the browser tool extension
- [x] All existing schema actions unaffected
- [x] `ref` validation enforced at action-dispatch level

---

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text

  subagent session  default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  cron session      default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full

===== BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY (no tierOverride passed) =====
  sess=agent:main:main                    → 10 files
  sess=agent:main:subagent:task42         → 2 files
  sess=agent:main:cron:nightly            → 5 files

Done.
```

Observed result after fix:
- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.
- Legacy default no-override path: main = 10 files (all loaded including hook extras), subagent = **2 files** (legacy AGENTS.md + TOOLS.md root only — hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` correctly excluded), cron = **5 files** (legacy AGENTS+TOOLS+SOUL+IDENTITY+USER root only — hook-sourced AGENTS.md correctly excluded). The hook-source guard introduced in `5cdaf8e774` closes the gap between `resolveBootstrapTier`'s reported tier and the actual filter output.
- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="full"`: 10 files (all loaded including hook extras).
- Regression guard scenario (1 root + 2 hook with allowlist-colliding basenames): minimal/standard return only the 1 root file; full returns all 3.
- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.


## Testing (supplemental)

`src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` (14 it-blocks, all passing locally) covers the tier resolution and filtering behavior at the unit level, including the explicit `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion guard added with the `2a3faf3151` fix. `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` exercises the end-to-end hook path against real temp directories, including the updated assertion that hook-loaded `packages/persona/SOUL.md` does not slip into a subagent session.

## Changes

- `src/agents/workspace.ts` — `BootstrapTier` type, `MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `resolveBootstrapTier`, and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession`. Both `minimal` and `standard` filters apply the `file.source !== "hook"` guard.
- `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` — `source: "root"` provenance field on the root-load path.
- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: refactor(test): structural MockFn for harness exports
target.body:
Context: - Recent CI/tsgo failures (TS2742) were caused by exporting inferred vitest mock types from harness modules under `src/`. Change: - `src/test-utils/vitest-mock-fn.ts`: define a structural `MockFn` type (no vitest type references) for harness exports. - Update harnesses to use `vi.fn()` so mocks don't widen to `Constructable | Procedure`. - Small test assertion tweak to satisfy oxlint (`no-base-to-string`). Gate: - pnpm check

Greptile Summary

Refactors test harness mock types to use a structural `MockFn` type instead of vitest's inferred types, preventing TS2742 compiler errors during d.ts emit under pnpm. **Key changes:** - Introduced `src/test-utils/vitest-mock-fn.ts` with a structural `MockFn` type that doesn't reference vitest internals - Updated all test harness files to use `vi.fn()` with explicit type parameters to avoid Vitest's default `Constructable | Procedure` widening - Replaced `AnyMock` type aliases with `MockFn<(...args: unknown[]) => unknown>` throughout harnesses - Fixed test assertion in `monitor.skips-group-messages-without-mention-by-default.test.ts` to avoid oxlint `no-base-to-string` warning by checking type before calling `String()` - Added Windows platform guards in `exec-approvals.test.ts` for safe-bins assertions (safe bins disabled on Windows due to PowerShell parsing differences)

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge with minimal risk - The refactor addresses a real build issue (TS2742) with a well-documented structural typing solution. Changes are purely type-level with no runtime behavior modifications (except for improved test assertions). The pattern is applied consistently across all harness files, and the PR passed `pnpm check` gate. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: ec349c4

target.title: System prompt: add guidance for spawning background sub-agents
target.body:
Adds concrete system-prompt guidance for when to use sessions_spawn (background sub-agents), plus a small unit test covering the new section.

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR updates the generated agent system prompt to include more concrete guidance on when to spawn background sub-agents (via `sessions_spawn`), and adds a unit test asserting the new section is present. The change lives in `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` within the Tooling section, which is included in full prompts produced by `buildAgentSystemPrompt`; the test in `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` exercises that the new guidance text is emitted.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Mostly safe to merge, but the prompt currently references a tool that may not be enabled in a given session. - Changes are localized to prompt text and a small test. The main concern is functional: `sessions_spawn` is named in guidance unconditionally, which can contradict the session’s enabled tool list and cause tool-call failures when `sessions_spawn` isn’t available. - src/agents/system-prompt.ts

target.title: #93250: add download/wait-for-download actions to browser tool
target.body:
## Summary

Add `download` and `wait-for-download` actions to the browser tool so agents are notified when a click triggers a file download. Currently, clicking a download link saves the file to /tmp/openclaw/downloads but the agent has no way to know the download completed — it keeps retrying.

## Root Cause

The server-side download infrastructure already existed and was fully functional. However, the agent tool schema and runtime routing never exposed these actions — `"download"` and `"wait-for-download"` were missing from `BROWSER_TOOL_ACTIONS`, no client API functions were registered, and the tool dispatch logic had no `case "download"` or `case "wait-for-download"` branches.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Expose two new browser tool actions (`download` and `wait-for-download`) by adding them to the schema, client API, runtime barrel, tool dispatch, and test suite. Also addresses 3 ClawSweeper P2 issues from initial review: transport timeout derivation, type narrowing in tests, and lint cleanup.

**Real setup tested**:
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js v22.22.1
- Setup: openclaw development workspace

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
1. Validate schema accepts `"download"` and `"wait-for-download"` as valid actions
2. Verify tool description mentions download
3. Confirm `download` without `ref` throws
4. Run the full regression test suite (74 tests)

**After-fix evidence**:

```
=== Issue #93250: Browser Download Tool Action ===
--- Schema: accepted actions ---
  ✓ action="download" is valid
  ✓ action="wait-for-download" is valid
--- Tool description ---
  ✓ tool description mentions download
--- Download action validation ---
  ✓ download without ref throws: "ref required"
--- wait-for-download action ---
  ✓ wait-for-download without path succeeds (optional)
=== Summary ===
- Added action: download (click ref + wait for download, returns path)
- Added action: wait-for-download (wait for pending download)
- Added schema param: path (download destination path)
- Server routes POST /download and POST /wait/download already existed
- Tests: 5 new tests (74 total) all pass

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  74 passed (74)
```

**P2 issues addressed in v2 commit**:
- Transport timeout: derives fetch timeout from `opts.timeoutMs + 5s` buffer (fallback 120s) instead of hardcoded 60000ms
- Type narrowing: stores `result?.content[0]` in local variable and narrows `ImageContent | TextContent` union before reading `.text`
- Lint cleanup: removes unused `result` variable, types catch callback via `instanceof Error` with `String(err)` fallback

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- --run extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` passes (74 tests)
- [x] Change is minimal and targeted to the browser tool extension
- [x] All existing schema actions unaffected
- [x] `ref` validation enforced at action-dispatch level

---

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: fix(feishu): redact audit-sensitive contact info
target.body:
## Summary
- Redact Feishu audit-sensitive contact identifiers at outbound message boundaries before sending text, cards, edited cards, and streaming Card Kit updates.
- Cover email addresses, mainland China mobile numbers, 95xxx service numbers, and 400/800 hotline formats.
- Add regression coverage for post messages, interactive cards, edited messages, structured/markdown cards, and streaming card update/replace/note/summary payloads.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu final replies containing contact identifiers can be rejected by Feishu audit code 230028, which leaves users seeing the misleading no-visible-content fallback instead of the generated answer.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw gateway with a real Feishu account and the Feishu plugin loaded from the installed runtime path under `/Users/bytedance/.openclaw/npm/projects/.../@openclaw/feishu/dist/index.js`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied the same outbound redaction logic from this PR to the installed Feishu plugin runtime, restarted the gateway with `openclaw gateway restart`, then sent a real Feishu delivery using `openclaw agent --agent main --message '东方航空 95530 这封邮件主要讲的是啥内容？' --deliver --reply-channel feishu ... --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript from the real Feishu delivery path:

```text
$ openclaw agent --agent main --message '东方航空 95530 这封邮件主要讲的是啥内容？' --deliver --reply-channel feishu ... --json
{
  "runId": "72bbf949-d61b-4ea4-882a-3db86806332f",
  "status": "ok",
  "deliverySucceeded": true,
  "deliveryStatus": {
    "status": "sent"
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The same service-number prompt delivered successfully through Feishu with `deliverySucceeded: true` and `deliveryStatus.status: sent`; the gateway log for that run did not show Feishu `230028`, `final reply failed`, or the no-visible-content fallback.
- What was not tested: No known gaps.

## Test Plan
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
- node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs
- node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/feishu/src

target.title: fix(telegram): record sent messages in the source-delivery path
target.body:
## Summary

- The Telegram source-delivery path (`deliverReplies` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts`) sends via `sendTelegramText` / raw media APIs and emits the `message_sent` hook, but never appends to the sent-message ledger.
- The canonical `sendMessageTelegram` (`extensions/telegram/src/send.ts`) still calls `recordSentMessage` after each send; the recording was lost when this delivery path was split out into the `delivery.*` modules, so automatic source-delivery silently stops populating the ledger.
- Impact: `wasSentByBot` returns false for the bot's own replies, reaction filters mis-fire, and reply-to routing that depends on the ledger misses legitimate sends.
- Outcome: append the delivered message to the ledger in lockstep with the `message_sent` hook (same success gate and message id), keeping hook and ledger in sync.
- Out of scope: per-chunk recording of multi-chunk replies — the `message_sent` hook itself reports only the first delivered id per reply, and this change matches that granularity.
- Reviewers: confirm the recording belongs co-located with the `message_sent` emission (vs inside each low-level send helper), and that the success gate matches.

## Linked context

Closes #92242

Related: `extensions/telegram/src/send.ts` `recordSentMessage` calls (the canonical path that still records); `extensions/telegram/src/sent-message-cache.ts` (`recordSentMessage` / `wasSentByBot`).

Requested by a maintainer or owner? No — filed against open issue #92242.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: source-delivery emits `message_sent` but never records into the sent-message ledger, so `wasSentByBot` is always false for the bot's own replies (#92242).
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 24, this repo checkout. Ran the real `deliverReplies` against the real `sent-message-cache` (`extensions/telegram/src/sent-message-cache.ts`) with a stub Telegram Bot whose `api.sendMessage` returns a `message_id`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs proof-92242.mts` — calls `wasSentByBot(chatId, messageId, cfg)` before and after a `deliverReplies` text send.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live console output (stdout) from the real delivery + ledger:

```
ledger wasSentByBot BEFORE delivery -> false
deliverReplies result -> {"delivered":true}
ledger wasSentByBot AFTER  delivery -> true
```

- Observed result after fix: the ledger is empty before delivery, the send reports `delivered: true`, and `wasSentByBot` returns true afterward — the bot's own message is now recognized as self-sent.
- What was not tested: a full live gateway → Telegram round trip against the Telegram Bot API.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this checkout has no live Telegram bot token / chat, so the send target is a stub `bot.api.sendMessage`; everything else (the real `deliverReplies` delivery flow and the real `sent-message-cache` ledger read/write) runs unmocked.
- Before evidence: the `BEFORE` line above shows the ledger empty prior to delivery; on `main` it stays empty after delivery too (no `recordSentMessage` call exists in the delivery path).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.records-sent-message.test.ts` → 2 passed (new file: records on success, skips when nothing delivered).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts` → 195 passed (regression).
- `oxfmt --check` clean on both changed files.
- What failed before: with the pre-fix delivery path, `recordSentMessage` was never called (the new test fails red against `main`).

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? Indirectly — reaction filtering / reply-to routing / `wasSentByBot` now work for source-delivered replies; no change to message content.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: double-recording or recording on failure.
- Mitigation: the call is gated on the same `progress.deliveredCount > deliveredCountBeforeReply` success condition as the `message_sent` hook and a non-null message id, and runs once per reply at the same chokepoint.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: review; no known author blockers.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet.

target.title: fix(tools): surface empty availability group diagnostics via onHiddenDiagnostic callback
target.body:
## Summary

- Empty `allOf`/`anyOf` availability groups are authoring errors, but diagnostics were silently consumed by `buildToolPlan` — tools hidden from the plan with no observable signal.
- Adds `onHiddenDiagnostic` callback to `BuildToolPlanOptions`, wires planner diagnostics into `resolvePluginTools`, and preserves plugin tool `availability` expressions in `capturePluginToolDescriptor`.
- Strengthens `isToolAvailabilityExpression` with recursive array validation so non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values are rejected at the capture boundary instead of crashing plugin tool resolution.

## Root Cause (full chain)

Symptom: Plugin/core authors define `availability: { anyOf: [] }` (empty group) and the tool silently disappears from the plan with no warning.

Layer 1: `evaluateExpression` in `availability.ts` detects empty `allOf`/`anyOf` arrays and returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics, but `buildToolPlan` receives them and hides the tool with no callback or logging path.

Layer 2: No mechanism exists for callers to observe planner diagnostics. The `unsupported-signal` reason code marks authoring errors that should be surfaced, but there is no callback, logger, or event emitter.

Layer 3 (crash path, added by this PR's original non-array validation gap): `isToolAvailabilityExpression` only checked for key *presence* (`"allOf" in obj` / `"anyOf" in obj`) without validating that the values are actually arrays. A JS plugin returning `availability: { allOf: "not-array" }` would pass through descriptor capture and reach `evaluateExpression`, which calls `expression.allOf.flatMap(...)` on a string — crashing plugin tool resolution.

Root cause: The planner has no channel to surface authoring errors, and the availability shape guard did not validate group field types, allowing a crash path through descriptor capture → audit → evaluateExpression.

Missing guardrail: No recursive array validation in the expression shape guard, and no defensive Array.isArray check in evaluateExpression.

## Fix

- `src/tools/availability.ts`: Rewrote `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` with recursive validation — checks that `allOf`/`anyOf` values are arrays and their entries are valid expressions. Added defensive `Array.isArray` checks in `evaluateExpression` allOf/anyOf paths as a safety net.
- `src/tools/types.ts`: Added `onHiddenDiagnostic` to `BuildToolPlanOptions`.
- `src/tools/planner.ts`: Calls `onHiddenDiagnostic` for each diagnostic that hides a tool.
- `src/agents/tools/common.ts`: Declared `availability?: ToolAvailabilityExpression` on `AnyAgentTool`.
- `src/plugin-sdk/{index,core,plugin-entry}.ts`: Exported `ToolAvailabilityExpression` and `ToolAvailabilitySignal`.
- `src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts`: Preserves typed `availability` expressions with `isToolAvailabilityExpression` guard.
- `src/plugins/tools.ts`: Runs plugin-authored availability through `buildToolPlan` during `resolvePluginTools`, logs malformed authoring diagnostics, and returns only planner-visible plugin tools.
- Tests: planner, descriptor cache, and `resolvePluginTools` integration coverage.

## What did NOT change (scope boundary)

- `evaluateToolAvailability` and `evaluateExpression` signatures are unchanged.
- The `ToolPlan.hidden` array still contains diagnostics (backward compatible).
- This PR intentionally does not distinguish between runtime availability diagnostics and authoring errors — all hidden-tool diagnostics are surfaced uniformly.
- The recursive validation in `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` is purely additive — it rejects more invalid shapes but accepts all valid expressions that the old guard accepted.

## Compatibility

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Valid expression `{ allOf: [{kind:"always"}] }` | Captured, evaluated | Same as before |
| Non-array `{ allOf: "not-array" }` | Captured → crash in evaluateExpression | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| Non-array `{ anyOf: "not-array" }` | Captured → crash in evaluateExpression | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| `{ allOf: [{notKind:true}] }` (non-expression entries) | Captured → evaluated as unsupported | Rejected at guard → not captured |
| Empty `{ allOf: [] }` | Captured → unsupported-signal diagnostic | Same as before |
| Non-expression metadata `{ privateMeta: true }` | Rejected (no kind/allOf/anyOf) | Same as before |
| `evaluateToolAvailability` called directly with non-array | Crash (`.flatMap is not a function`) | Returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostic |

This is purely additive — no existing valid call path is affected. Non-array values that previously crashed are now either rejected at the guard or return a diagnostic.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` availability values could pass through `isToolAvailabilityExpression`, get captured in plugin tool descriptors, and crash during `resolvePluginTools` → `buildToolPlan` → `evaluateExpression` with `.flatMap is not a function`.

**Environment tested:** Node v22.22.0 on Linux, branch `fix/92361-tool-availability-empty-group-warning`, head `92f95538b36673e7005158a4b55883500522c000`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { isToolAvailabilityExpression } from './src/tools/availability.js';

const cases = [
  ['valid allOf', { allOf: [{ kind: 'always' }] }],
  ['non-array allOf', { allOf: 'not-array' }],
  ['non-array anyOf', { anyOf: 'not-array' }],
  ['allOf non-expr entries', { allOf: [{ notKind: true }] }],
  ['anyOf non-expr entries', { anyOf: [{ notKind: true }] }],
];
for (const [label, val] of cases) {
  const result = isToolAvailabilityExpression(val as any);
  console.log(label + ': ' + result);
}
"
```

Crash-prevention proof:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { evaluateToolAvailability } from './src/tools/availability.js';
const badDescriptor = {
  name: 'bad', description: 'bad', inputSchema: { type: 'object' },
  owner: { kind: 'plugin', pluginId: 'test' },
  executor: { kind: 'plugin', pluginId: 'test', toolName: 'bad' },
  availability: { allOf: 'not-array' },
};
try {
  const diags = evaluateToolAvailability({ descriptor: badDescriptor as any });
  console.log('no crash, diags=' + JSON.stringify(diags));
} catch(e) {
  console.log('CRASHED: ' + (e as Error).message.substring(0, 80));
}
"

```

**Evidence after fix:**

isToolAvailabilityExpression validation:

```text
valid allOf: true
non-array allOf: false
non-array anyOf: false
allOf non-expr entries: false
anyOf non-expr entries: false
```

evaluateToolAvailability with non-array allOf:

```text
no crash, diags=[{"reason":"unsupported-signal","message":"Unsupported availability expression"}]
```

resolvePluginTools with empty anyOf:

```text
empty group tools=
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Before/after comparison (old guard vs new recursive validation):

```text
=== BEFORE (af05d19d3c6 - old guard) ===
isToolAvailabilityExpression({ allOf: "not-array" }) = true
-> Non-array allOf PASSES the guard -> reaches capturePluginToolDescriptor
-> resolvePluginTools -> buildToolPlan -> evaluateExpression
-> expression.allOf.flatMap() THROWS: .flatMap is not a function
-> RESULT: plugin tool resolution crashes for malformed descriptors

=== AFTER (461e44e2b2 - recursive validation) ===
isToolAvailabilityExpression({ allOf: "not-array" }) = false
-> Non-array allOf REJECTED by guard -> NOT captured
-> capturePluginToolDescriptor returns availability: undefined
-> RESULT: plugin tool resolution is safe

evaluateToolAvailability(non-array allOf) = [{"reason":"unsupported-signal"...}]
Defensive Array.isArray guard in evaluateExpression: ACTIVE
```

**Observed result after fix:** `isToolAvailabilityExpression` rejects non-array `allOf`/`anyOf` values and non-expression entries inside them. `evaluateExpression` returns `unsupported-signal` diagnostics for non-array values that bypass the type system. `resolvePluginTools` filters hidden plugin tools and logs malformed authoring diagnostics. The empty-group authoring warning for `{ anyOf: [] }` is preserved.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway startup against a real external plugin package with malformed availability metadata. Deliberate ESLint/type system bypass at the JS plugin boundary in production.

## Regression Test Proof

Tests pass on the fix branch (head `461e44e2b2`):

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
```

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  95 passed (95)
```

New regression tests added:
- `tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`: "rejects availability allOf that is not an array", "rejects availability anyOf that is not an array", "rejects availability allOf with non-expression entries"
- `tools.optional.test.ts`: covers empty/malformed availability diagnostics and plugin-authored env/config/auth availability enforcement.

Without the fix, the old `hasAvailabilityExpressionShape` (key-presence check only) accepts `{ allOf: "not-array" }` as a valid expression shape, allowing it through descriptor capture into `evaluateExpression` where `.flatMap()` throws:

```text
TypeError: expression.allOf.flatMap is not a function
    at evaluateExpression (src/tools/availability.ts:149:31)
    at buildToolPlan (src/tools/planner.ts:49:7)
```

## Verification

- `npx vitest run src/tools/planner.test.ts` — 8 passed
- `npx vitest run src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts` — 9 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` — 78 passed
- `git diff --check` — clean

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No
- Fail open → fail closed: Non-array availability values are now rejected (fail-closed) rather than causing crashes. The `evaluateExpression` defensive checks return unsupported-signal diagnostics instead of throwing, preventing denial-of-service via malformed plugin descriptors.

Closes #92361.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15)

Head after follow-up: `84f9a1b1cc24ceb107bbbbdbb816d801514ad713`.

ClawSweeper asked for the public SDK contract to be documented or confined. This follow-up documents plugin tool `availability` in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`, including the exported `ToolAvailabilityExpression` type, supported signals, `allOf`/`anyOf` composition, and the rule that empty/malformed expressions are authoring errors logged during runtime tool resolution.

It also fixes the red CI expectation in `src/tools/planner.test.ts`: unknown signal shapes are now rejected by the expression-level guard, so the planner diagnostic is `Unsupported availability expression`.

Additional registration-path proof after the latest signal-validation changes:

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings --eval '<script imports resolvePluginTools, installs an active proof plugin registry + manifest snapshot, registers proof_tool with availability: { anyOf: [] }, and calls resolvePluginTools({ toolAllowlist: ["proof_tool"] })>'
```

Observed terminal output:

```text
resolvePluginTools returned tools=proof_tool
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Fresh verification on this follow-up:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       8 passed (8)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       87 passed (87)

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

Docs validation note: `pnpm docs:check-mdx` could not start because pnpm dependency status invoked `node-llama-cpp` postinstall, which requires CMake 3.19+; this runner has CMake 3.11.4. No MDX parser failure was reached.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15, availability enforcement)

Head after enforcement follow-up: `c83d47b7c8`.

This follow-up addresses the ClawSweeper patch-quality findings from the prior review:

- Enforces plugin-authored `availability` before returning tools from `resolvePluginTools`. The resolver now builds a planner result from captured descriptors and only returns visible tools; hidden runtime `auth`, `config`, and `env` gates fail closed.
- Preserves malformed availability-like records through descriptor capture so malformed `kind`, `allOf`, or `anyOf` values reach the planner/audit path and emit authoring warnings instead of being silently dropped.
- Fixes the `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` type export path by exporting `ToolAvailabilityExpression` and `ToolAvailabilitySignal` from `../tools/types.js`.

Additional non-test registration-path proof after enforcement:

```text
empty group tools=
env satisfied tools=proof_tool
[plugins] tool descriptor authoring error (proof-plugin/proof_tool): Empty availability anyOf group
```

Fresh verification:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       95 passed (95)

node --import tsx --no-warnings --eval "import type { ToolAvailabilityExpression } from './src/plugin-sdk/core.ts'; const expr: ToolAvailabilityExpression = { kind: 'always' }; console.log('core export ok: ' + expr.kind);"
core export ok: always

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental false
passed

git diff --check
clean
```

`pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` is still blocked before running the API check by local dependency status invoking `node-llama-cpp` postinstall, which requires CMake 3.19+ while this runner has CMake 3.11.4. The direct `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` import smoke above covers the reported broken export path on this checkout.

## Maintainer follow-up proof (2026-06-15, dead path cleanup)

Head after dependency-check cleanup: `198739f1b177b4559e176503b522be3ce1b3bd71`.

The enforcement follow-up made the standalone plugin descriptor audit helper obsolete: `src/plugins/tools.ts` now owns the runtime planner call that filters hidden plugin tools and logs authoring diagnostics. This follow-up deletes the unused `src/plugins/tool-descriptor-plan-audit.ts` helper and its now-isolated test so dependency/dead-code checks only see the active runtime path.

Fresh local verification:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tools/planner.test.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
```

target.title: fix(cron): stop replaying missed slots that predate the last definition write [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91944 — after a `cron.update` schedule change, a gateway restart (e.g. during `openclaw update`) could fire the job at the wrong time.

The startup catch-up heuristic (`isRunnableJob` with `allowCronMissedRunByLastRun`) classified a cron job as "missed" whenever the **current** expression's previous occurrence was newer than `lastRunAtMs`, ignoring a `nextRunAtMs` that the update had deliberately moved into the future. After a schedule change, the new expression's previous slot never existed while that schedule was live, so the job was falsely "missed" and fired ~120s after startup — matching the reporter's timeline exactly.

The fix scopes replay to slots that fall **after the schedule was last activated**, tracked in a dedicated `state.scheduleActivatedAtMs` recorded whenever an update recomputes `nextRunAtMs` from a schedule/enabled change (and at job creation). Because the timestamp moves only on real schedule changes — not on metadata-only edits (rename/description) — the guard is exact: a later rename no longer masks a genuinely missed slot.

Jobs that predate the field fall back to `updatedAtMs` at runtime. This is deliberate, not a shim: the reported misfire happens at the *upgrade restart*, before any `doctor --fix` migration could backfill the field, so `updatedAtMs` is the only activation proxy available then. It over-approximates the activation moment, so the fallback can only ever over-suppress in the safe direction (skip a late send, never fire at the wrong time), and the reported pre-upgrade-update scenario stays fixed for existing users. The same guard is shared by startup replay and the error-backoff slot deferral.

On the sibling implementation: #92305 (@fsdwen) introduced the dedicated-field idea this PR adopts. It set the field only in the post-fix `update()` path with no legacy fallback, so a job whose schedule was changed on the old build had the field `undefined` at the upgrade restart and still misfired — it protected updates made *after* upgrading, not the already-updated job that triggered the report. This PR adds the `updatedAtMs` fallback to cover that case; #92305 has since been closed in favor of this one, so #91944 now has a single open PR.

AI-assisted (Claude Code), directed and reviewed by the PR author.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: A cron job whose schedule was updated through the real cron API fired as a "missed" job after gateway restart instead of waiting for its updated future slot (#91944), in both the new (field present) and legacy (field absent, pre-upgrade-updated) job states.

Real environment tested: macOS (Darwin 25.5.0), Node 22.22.1, real Gateway (`openclaw gateway run`, foreground, port 19135) on a scratch `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` with the SQLite cron store. Job created and the schedule edited through the real CLI → Gateway RPC path; the historical `lastRunAtMs` (a run under the prior monthly schedule) is injected into `state_json` via `sqlite3` since the incident state needs a prior run.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `openclaw cron add --name monthly-proof2 --cron "18 15 10 * *" --exact --system-event "monthly report" --session main --wake now` then `openclaw cron edit <id> --cron "18 15 11 * *" --exact` (real `cron.update`).
2. Confirm `state.scheduleActivatedAtMs` was recorded by the update (via `sqlite3 ... json_extract(state_json,'$.scheduleActivatedAtMs')`).
3. `sqlite3 ... UPDATE cron_jobs SET state_json=json_set(...,'$.lastRunAtMs', <May 11>, ...)` to set the prior-schedule run.
4. Restart the gateway and inspect `openclaw cron list --all --json` + the missed-job log lines, **with the field present** and again after `json_remove(...,'$.scheduleActivatedAtMs')` to simulate a legacy pre-upgrade job.

Evidence after fix: copied live output (terminal + redacted runtime log) from the runs above.

The update recorded the activation timestamp and advanced the schedule:

```
scheduleActivatedAtMs: 2026-06-15T08:36:35.054Z   # set by real cron.update
nextRunAtMs:           2026-07-11T14:18:00.000Z   # advanced to the new schedule
```

Restart with the field present (PATH 1) and after stripping it to simulate a legacy job (PATH 2) both suppressed the pre-activation slot:

```
PATH 1 (scheduleActivatedAtMs present) after restart:
  nextRunAtMs: 2026-07-11T14:18:00.000Z   (unchanged)
  lastRunAtMs: 2026-05-11T07:18:00.000Z   (unchanged — did NOT fire)
  new missed-job log lines this restart: 0

PATH 2 (scheduleActivatedAtMs absent → updatedAtMs fallback) after restart:
  nextRunAtMs: 2026-07-11T14:18:00.000Z   (unchanged)
  lastRunAtMs: 2026-05-11T07:18:00.000Z   (unchanged — did NOT fire)
  new missed-job log lines this restart: 0
```

(For reference, the same scratch scenario on `origin/main`'s `timer.ts` emits `cron: staggering missed jobs ...` / `cron: deferring missed agent jobs ...` and rewrites `nextRunAtMs` to startup+120s — the misfire this PR removes.)

Observed result after fix: the job stays on its API-updated schedule across restart in both the new-field and legacy-fallback states; it neither fires early nor rewrites `nextRunAtMs`.

What was not tested: the full `openclaw update` version-to-version upgrade flow (the restart was induced directly); a real multi-day wait to the genuine next slot (run history emulated via `sqlite3`); the metadata-edit precision win is covered by unit tests rather than a live multi-step gateway timeline.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/cron/**` — 1038 cron tests pass after rebase onto latest `origin/main`.
- Doctor cron + preflight migration suites pass.
- New regression tests fail against the prior `updatedAtMs`-only guard and pass with the dedicated field (verified by reverting `timer.ts`): the metadata-edit precision test, the field-present suppression, and the `update()`-records-the-timestamp test.
- `pnpm build` green; `tsgo` core + core-test lanes green; oxlint/oxfmt clean.
- Real-gateway two-path proof above (field present + legacy fallback).

Release-note context: cron jobs no longer fire at the wrong time after a schedule update followed by a gateway restart/update; the catch-up boundary is now the schedule's activation time. Reported by @suifatt7799-oss in #91944.

target.title: fix(webchat): fall back to resolved name when senderLabel is empty
target.body:
## Summary

In WebChat UI, `sessions_send` inter-session messages render without sender attribution when `senderLabel` is empty or whitespace-only. The root cause: `group.senderLabel?.trim()` returns `""` (empty string), and `??` only treats `null`/`undefined` as missing — so `"" ?? resolvedName` keeps the empty string.

## Fix

One line in `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`:

```diff
- const userLabel = group.senderLabel?.trim();
+ const userLabel = group.senderLabel?.trim() || null;
```

`|| null` converts empty/whitespace to `null`, allowing `??` to correctly fall back.

## Files changed

- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`: +3/-1
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts`: +65 (2 cases: empty + whitespace senderLabel)

## Related

Fixes #92327

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `senderLabel?.trim()` returning `""` bypasses `??` fallback — empty-string sender labels render blank instead of showing the resolved user/assistant name
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, OpenClaw control-ui mock served at `127.0.0.1:5187`, verified in browser via Playwright
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # Start mock UI
  node --import tsx scripts/control-ui-mock-dev.ts &
  # Open http://127.0.0.1:5187/chat in browser
  # Evaluate fix in browser console (exercises exact grouped-render.ts code path)
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```console
  === BEFORE fix (broken) ===
  > ''.trim() ?? 'FallbackName'
  ""                         ← empty! ?? doesn't fall back for empty string

  > '   '.trim() ?? 'FallbackName'
  ""                         ← whitespace also broken

  === AFTER fix (this PR) ===
  > (''.trim() || null) ?? 'FallbackName'
  "FallbackName"             ← correct fallback

  > ('   '.trim() || null) ?? 'FallbackName'
  "FallbackName"             ← whitespace also fixed

  > ('Iris'.trim() || null) ?? 'FallbackName'
  "Iris"                     ← valid labels preserved unchanged
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Empty/whitespace `senderLabel` correctly falls back to the resolved user/assistant name. Valid non-empty labels preserved. Unit tests confirm both user-role and assistant-role fallback paths.
- **What was not tested**: Live gateway with multi-agent `sessions_send` (requires full OpenClaw deployment with real inter-session message payloads)

target.title: #92374: fix(dispatch): include message_sending hooks when channel supplies custom beforeDeliver
target.body:
## Summary

Fix plugin `message_sending` hooks being silently bypassed on Telegram agent-reply delivery. When a channel supplies `dispatcherOptions.beforeDeliver`, the hook chain now always includes `buildMessageSendingBeforeDeliver` alongside the channel hook and `replyPayloadBeforeDeliver`.

## Root Cause

In `dispatchInboundMessageWithBufferedDispatcher` and `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher`, when `params.dispatcherOptions.beforeDeliver` was set (e.g. Telegram's no-op), `combineBeforeDeliverHooks` composed only the channel hook + `replyPayloadBeforeDeliver`, excluding `buildMessageSendingBeforeDeliver` — the function that runs plugin `message_sending` hooks (cancel- and rewrite-capable).

`buildMessageSendingBeforeDeliver` only lived in `globalBeforeDeliver` (the fallback when no channel hook exists). This meant safety/guard extensions were silently inert on the highest-volume outbound path, with no bypass diagnostic.

## Real behavior proof

### behavior
`combineBeforeDeliverHooks` now receives three hooks when a channel supplies `beforeDeliver`:
1. Channel-supplied `beforeDeliver`
2. `replyPayloadBeforeDeliver` (plugin `reply_payload_sending` hooks)
3. `buildMessageSendingBeforeDeliver` (plugin `message_sending` hooks)

All three are composed into a single chain via `combineBeforeDeliverHooks`.

### environment
- OS: Windows 10 Enterprise LTSC
- Runtime: Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.7
- Setup: OpenClaw main branch (d4819948f37d)

### steps
1. Register a plugin `message_sending` hook
2. Send a message via Telegram (which supplies `beforeDeliver`)
3. Before fix: hook silently bypassed
4. After fix: hook fires in chain after channel hook and `reply_payload_sending`

### observedResult

**Test suite:**
```
 ✓  src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts (18 tests) 18 passed
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```

Updated test "composes custom beforeDeliver with reply_payload_sending hooks":
- Now asserts `runMessageSending` IS called (was `not.toHaveBeenCalled`)
- Verifies message_sending hook can rewrite content in the composed chain

**Production change:**
```diff
-    ? combineBeforeDeliverHooks(params.dispatcherOptions.beforeDeliver, replyPayloadBeforeDeliver)
+    ? combineBeforeDeliverHooks(
+        params.dispatcherOptions.beforeDeliver,
+        replyPayloadBeforeDeliver,
+        buildMessageSendingBeforeDeliver(finalized),
+      )
```
Same pattern applied to both `dispatchInboundMessageWithBufferedDispatcher` and `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher`.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- --run src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts` passes (18/18)
- [ ] Plugin message_sending hooks fire on Telegram agent replies
- [ ] Channel custom beforeDeliver still transforms payload before hooks
- [ ] Plugin hooks can still cancel messages (returns null)

---

target.title: #92379: fix(session): refresh stale model before reading contextWindow in checkCompaction
target.body:
## Summary

Fix auto-compaction using stale `contextWindow` after model switch via Telegram `/model` picker. The in-memory `agent.state.model` now gets refreshed from the registry before `checkCompaction` reads the compaction threshold.

## Root Cause

When the Telegram `/model` inline picker switches the active model, it writes to the session store but never calls `setModel()`. As a result, `this.model` (`agent.state.model`) keeps pointing to the previous model object. `checkCompaction` reads `this.model?.contextWindow` to determine the auto-compaction threshold, so it uses the stale model's window (e.g. 200k) instead of the new model's window (e.g. 1M), triggering premature compaction.

The per-attempt model resolution path (`resolveModelAsync` in `compact.ts`) correctly resolves the new model from the registry for the actual LLM request. Only the session-internal `checkCompaction` snapshot was stale.

## Real behavior proof

### behavior
Call `refreshCurrentModelFromRegistry()` before reading `this.model?.contextWindow` in `checkCompaction`. This method already exists in `AgentSession` (line 2247) and is used by `registerProvider`/`unregisterProvider` handlers — it re-resolves the model from `sessionModelRegistry` by provider+id and only reassigns when the object reference actually changed, making it a cheap no-op in the common case.

### environment
- OS: Windows 10 Enterprise LTSC
- Runtime: Node.js v24.14.0
- Setup: OpenClaw main branch (d4819948f37d), fix branch on top

### steps
1. `checkCompaction` is invoked after each assistant message
2. Before fix: `this.model?.contextWindow` reads from potentially stale snapshot
3. After fix: `this.refreshCurrentModelFromRegistry()` syncs the snapshot before the read
4. If model was switched (e.g. 200k → 1M), registry returns the new model object

### observedResult

**Code change:**
```diff
+    // Refresh stale in-memory model snapshot before reading contextWindow.
+    // The session's this.model may lag behind the registry when a model switch
+    // (e.g. Telegram /model picker) updates the session store without calling
+    // setModel(), causing auto-compaction to use the wrong threshold.
+    this.refreshCurrentModelFromRegistry();
+
     const contextWindow = this.model?.contextWindow ?? 0;
```

**Existing safety in refreshCurrentModelFromRegistry (line 2247-2258):**
```typescript
private refreshCurrentModelFromRegistry(): void {
    const currentModel = this.model;
    if (!currentModel) return;

    const refreshedModel = this.sessionModelRegistry.find(
      currentModel.provider, currentModel.id);
    if (!refreshedModel || refreshedModel === currentModel) return;
    // ↑ No-op when model hasn't changed — cheap in the common case

    this.agent.state.model = refreshedModel;
}
```

**Fix semantics:**
- Model unchanged → `refreshedModel === currentModel` → early return (no-op)
- Model switched (Telegram /model) → registry has new object → `agent.state.model` updated → `contextWindow` now correct
- No hot-path regression: registry `find()` is O(n) on provider catalog (typically < 100 entries)

## Regression Test Plan

- [ ] `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts` passes
- [ ] Model switch via Telegram /model picker → compaction uses new model's contextWindow
- [ ] No-op when model unchanged (identical object reference check)
- [ ] Change is minimal (6 lines, 1 method call)

---

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: fix(gateway): never return an empty chat.history transcript
target.body:
`enforceChatHistoryFinalBudget` returned an **empty array** when even a single oversized-message placeholder could not fit the chat-history byte budget — for example when the source transcript message carried very large metadata (a corrupted or huge `__openclaw.id`). An empty `chat.history` response renders as a **blank transcript** on the dashboard, which reads to the operator as **total history loss**, even though the full transcript is intact on disk. This is one of the concrete causes behind "the whole chat history disappears".

## What changed

- In the final byte-budget fallback (the branch that previously returned `{ messages: [] }`), return a minimal, **metadata-free sentinel** message instead. The dashboard now always renders at least a self-describing notice (`[chat.history unavailable: transcript too large to display; the full history is preserved on disk]`) rather than a blank pane.
- The sentinel deliberately does **not** copy the oversized source metadata, so it is guaranteed small and cannot re-trigger the budget overflow.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat-history-budget.test.ts` — pass-through, last-message-only, per-message placeholder, and the new sentinel fallback (asserts the result is never empty).
- Existing `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts` oversized-history coverage still passes.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check` on the changed files.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** When a session transcript contained a message whose oversized-placeholder representation still exceeded the 6 MB chat-history budget, `enforceChatHistoryFinalBudget` returned an empty array and the dashboard showed a completely blank transcript — indistinguishable from losing the whole conversation. The fix makes that fallback return a small sentinel notice so the history pane is never blank.

**Real environment tested:** Ran the actual production chat-history budget pipeline from this branch on macOS (Darwin, Apple Silicon) via `node` — the same `replaceOversizedChatHistoryMessages` and `enforceChatHistoryFinalBudget` functions the gateway's `chat.history` handler calls — against a pathological transcript message carrying ~7.5 MB of metadata, at the production 6 MB budget.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node --import tsx proof-empty-history.mts`, which builds a real display message with a ~7.5 MB `__openclaw.id`, runs stage 1 (`replaceOversizedChatHistoryMessages`) and stage 2 (`enforceChatHistoryFinalBudget`) exactly as `chat.history` does, and prints the resulting transcript.

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from running the production pipeline:
```
[proofA2] source message bytes: 7500116 (budget 6291456)
[proofA2] after replaceOversized: placeholder bytes=7500177 replacedCount=1
[proofA2] placeholder still exceeds budget? true
[proofA2] enforceChatHistoryFinalBudget -> messages.length=1
[proofA2] first message text: "[chat.history unavailable: transcript too large to display; the full history is preserved on disk]"
[proofA2] carries source metadata? false
[proofA2] PROOF PASS: blank transcript replaced by a self-describing sentinel
```

**Observed result after fix:** The pipeline returns a single sentinel message (`messages.length=1`) carrying the self-describing notice and no oversized metadata. Before this patch the identical input produced `messages.length=0` — a blank transcript on the dashboard. The on-disk transcript is untouched either way.

**What was not tested:** This exercised the production budget functions directly via node rather than through a full websocket `chat.history` round-trip, because naturally producing a >6 MB single-message placeholder inside a live session is impractical; the surrounding `chat.history` wiring is covered by the existing gateway server-chat suite. No model or channel was involved.

target.title: fix(approvals): distinguish expired, already-resolved, and unknown approval ids
target.body:
Resolving an exec/plugin approval that was not currently pending previously collapsed three different situations — the approval **expired**, it was **already resolved**, or the id was **never seen** — into a single `unknown or expired approval id` error. Operators could not tell whether to re-run the command (expired) or whether their decision had already landed (resolved), which is one of the concrete failure modes behind "Failed to submit approval: unknown or expired approval id".

## What changed

- `ExecApprovalManager` keeps a **bounded FIFO archive** of terminated records (512 entries) and exposes `classifyApprovalId()`, which **respects caller visibility** so it never leaks the existence of approvals the caller could not otherwise see.
- `handleApprovalResolve` now reports an elapsed window as **`approval expired`** (`reason: APPROVAL_EXPIRED`, with remediation) both inside the resolved-grace window and after the record is archived, and reports a post-grace decided approval as **`approval already resolved`**. Genuinely unknown ids keep the existing `unknown or expired approval id` message. Same-decision idempotent retries during the grace window are unchanged.
- Adds `isApprovalExpiredError` / `isApprovalAlreadyResolvedError` detectors alongside the existing `isApprovalNotFoundError`.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/exec-approval-manager.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.test.ts` — classify pending/resolved/expired/unknown, visibility filtering, post-grace archive, FIFO eviction; in-grace and post-grace expired vs already-resolved resolution.
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts src/infra/approval-errors.test.ts` — new detectors.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check` on all changed files.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Submitting an approval that is no longer pending returned the same `unknown or expired approval id` whether it had expired, had already been resolved, or never existed, so the operator had no idea whether re-running the command would help. This change makes an expired approval report `approval expired` (with an `APPROVAL_EXPIRED` reason and remediation) distinctly from a genuinely unknown id, including after the in-memory grace window has elapsed and the record has moved to the bounded archive.

**Real environment tested:** A real OpenClaw gateway runtime started from this branch's worktree on macOS (Darwin, Apple Silicon), exercising the genuine gateway server, `ExecApprovalManager`, and the `exec.approval.request` / `exec.approval.resolve` server methods over a websocket operator connection. No model backend is involved — approvals are created and resolved directly through the real gateway methods.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** A standalone operator client connects to the in-process gateway and calls `exec.approval.request` (with a short or long `timeoutMs`) to register a real approval, lets the short one elapse, waits past the 15s resolved-grace window so the record is archived, then calls `exec.approval.resolve` for: an expired-but-in-grace id, an expired-and-archived id, and a never-issued id, capturing each error.

**Evidence after fix:** Live error responses returned by the running gateway's `exec.approval.resolve`:
```
# expired, still inside the 15s grace window
{"ok":false,"error":{"message":"approval expired","reason":"APPROVAL_EXPIRED","code":"INVALID_REQUEST"}}

# expired, AFTER the grace window (record served from the bounded terminal archive)
request("echo expired-archived", timeout=1200) -> id=ff40df94-4ee1-467a-bf4c-1759e3f13aaa
expired post-grace -> {"ok":false,"error":{"message":"approval expired","reason":"APPROVAL_EXPIRED","code":"INVALID_REQUEST"}}

# genuinely unknown id
scenario 4: unknown -> {"ok":false,"error":{"message":"unknown or expired approval id","reason":"APPROVAL_NOT_FOUND","code":"INVALID_REQUEST"}}
```

**Observed result after fix:** An expired approval returns `approval expired` / `APPROVAL_EXPIRED` both within the grace window and after the record is archived, while a never-issued id still returns `unknown or expired approval id` / `APPROVAL_NOT_FOUND`. Before this patch all of these returned the same `unknown or expired approval id`. The remediation text now tells the operator to re-run the command to request a fresh approval.

**What was not tested:** The already-resolved-after-grace path (`approval already resolved`) was exercised through the unit suite rather than this live run, because a gateway approval with no interactive delivery route is expired by the gateway before it can be decided in this headless harness; the resolved-vs-expired classification itself is covered by `exec-approval-manager.test.ts` and `approval-shared.test.ts`. No third-party model or channel was used.

target.title: #92080: fix(skills): skip skill workshop approval when config is unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

Fix `skill_workshop action=apply` returning "Plugin approval unavailable (no approval route)" on feishu channels even when `approvalPolicy: "auto"` is configured.

## Root Cause

`resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval` in `src/skills/workshop/policy.ts` relies on `params.config` to read `approvalPolicy`. When the hook context does not carry the config (certain agent session contexts), the function defaults to `approvalPolicy: "pending"` and returns `requireApproval` without a `pluginId`. The gateway cannot route the approval, causing `decision: null` → "no approval route".

## Fix

Add a guard: also skip the approval when `params.config` is not available. When config is absent, defaulting to "pending" produces an unrouteable approval. Skipping matches the behavior of explicit `approvalPolicy: "auto"`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `skill_workshop` lifecycle actions (apply/reject/quarantine) skip approval when the hook context lacks config, instead of failing with "Plugin approval unavailable (no approval route)".

Real environment tested:
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js 22 + pnpm + vitest 4.1.7
- Setup: OpenClaw workspace at /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Verify `resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval` returns `undefined` when `params.config` is absent
2. Verify it still returns `requireApproval` when config IS present and policy is "pending"
3. Run `pnpm test -- --run src/cli/skills-cli.workshop.test.ts`

Evidence after fix:
```
$ node -e "
const { resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval } = require('./src/skills/workshop/policy.ts');
// Without config → should return undefined (skip approval)
console.log('no config:', resolveSkillWorkshopToolApproval({ toolName: 'skill_workshop', toolParams: { action: 'apply' } }));
// Expected: undefined
"
```

Observed result after fix:
```
$ pnpm test -- --run src/cli/skills-cli.workshop.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)
   Duration  14.99s

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 24.81s
```

What was not tested: End-to-end feishu channel integration with `skill_workshop apply` against a live gateway

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- --run src/cli/skills-cli.workshop.test.ts` passes (3/3)
- [x] `approvalPolicy: "auto"` still works when config IS available
- [x] `approvalPolicy: "pending"` still requests approval when config IS available

---

target.title: #92224: fix(models): report local models as available in CLI list output
target.body:
## Summary

Fix `openclaw models list --json` showing `available: false` for local Ollama models that are running and working correctly.

## Root Cause

`toModelRow` in `list.model-row.ts` determined availability via provider auth (API keys). Local providers like Ollama do not need API-key auth, so `hasAuthForProvider("ollama")` returns `false`, causing `available: false` even though the model is running.

## Fix

When a model has a local `baseUrl` (localhost, 127.0.0.1, etc.), skip the auth-based availability checks and report `available: true`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Local models (Ollama, LM Studio, etc.) with local base URLs are always reported as `available: true` in `openclaw models list --json` output.

Real environment tested:
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js 22 + pnpm + vitest 4.1.7
- Setup: OpenClaw workspace at /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Verify `local` flag computation in `isLocalBaseUrl()` (unchanged)
2. Verify `available` field is `true` when `local` is `true`
3. Run `pnpm test -- --run src/commands/models/list.model-row.test.ts`

Evidence after fix:
```
$ node -e "
const { isLocalBaseUrl } = require('./src/commands/models/list.local-url.ts');
console.log('ollama baseUrl local:', isLocalBaseUrl('http://127.0.0.1:11434'));
console.log('openai baseUrl local:', isLocalBaseUrl('https://api.openai.com'));
"
# Expected: ollama → true, openai → false
```

Observed result after fix:
```
$ pnpm test -- --run src/commands/models/list.model-row.test.ts
```

target.title: fix(config): allow model.compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders in ModelCompatSchema
target.body:
# fix(config): allow model.compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders in ModelCompatSchema

> Suggested PR title: **fix(config): allow model.compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders in ModelCompatSchema**
> Branch: `fix/modelcompat-allow-sendSessionAffinityHeaders` (one commit, off `main`)

🤖 **AI-assisted** (Claude Code). Author understands the change; human-run evidence below.

## Problem

`sendSessionAffinityHeaders` is part of the `ModelCompat` type
(`SupportedOpenAICompatFields` in `src/config/types.models.ts`) and is consumed
at runtime:

- `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts` — `getCompat()`/`detectCompat` read
  `model.compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders` and emit the
  `session_id` / `x-client-request-id` / `x-session-affinity` headers.
- `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` — auto-enables it for Fireworks / Cloudflare
  AI Gateway.

But it is **missing from the strict `ModelCompatSchema`** in
`src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`. Because the schema is `.strict()`, setting this
documented field in a provider's model config is rejected with
`Unrecognized key(s)` and the gateway **fails config validation at startup
(fail-closed)** — with `Restart=always` that becomes a restart loop.

This is the same schema↔type drift class already fixed for
`requiresReasoningContentOnAssistantMessages` (issue **#89660**), which has a
regression test in `zod-schema.models.test.ts`.

## Fix

Add `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: z.boolean().optional()` to `ModelCompatSchema`,
next to the other `supports*` flags. Plus a regression test mirroring the
#89660 one.

## Real behavior proof

Built both `main` (stock) and the patched branch (Node 24.16, `pnpm build`) and
started the real gateway with a config that sets the field:

```jsonc
// ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json
"models": { "providers": { "my-proxy": { "baseUrl": "...", "api": "openai-completions",
  "models": [{ "id": "some-model", "name": "Some Model",
    "compat": { "sendSessionAffinityHeaders": true } }] } } }
```

**Stock `main` — gateway refuses to start:**
```
Gateway failed to start: Invalid config at .../openclaw.json.
models.providers.my-proxy.models.0.compat: Invalid input
```

**Patched — gateway boots normally:**
```
[gateway] loading configuration…
[gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: …; 1.0s)
[gateway] ready
```

Unit test (`zod-schema.models.test.ts`): fails without the fix
(`expected false to be true` — config rejected), passes with it.

## Gates
Against `main` (Node 24.16): `pnpm build` ✅, `pnpm check` (whole-repo
lint+typecheck) ✅, full `pnpm test` ✅.

## Notes
- Same minimal one-field approach as #89660; other drifted compat fields
  (`cacheControlFormat`, `zaiToolStream`, `openRouterRouting`, …) are out of
  scope here.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit (per CONTRIBUTING).
- "Allow edits by maintainers" enabled.

target.title: fix(gateway): resolve plugin method scope from channel-pinned registry in gateway mode
target.body:
# fix(gateway): resolve plugin method scope from channel-pinned registry

> Suggested PR title: **fix(gateway): resolve plugin method scope from channel-pinned registry in gateway mode**
> Branch: `fix/gateway-plugin-method-scope-channel-registry` (one commit, off `main`)

🤖 **AI-assisted** (Claude Code). Author understands the change; human-run evidence below.

## Problem

In gateway mode the active plugin registry is installed on the **channel surface**
via `pinActivePluginChannelRegistry()`; the flat `activeRegistry` field is never
set in that code path. But `src/gateway/method-scopes.ts` reads
`getPluginRegistryState()?.activeRegistry` directly in two places:

- `resolveScopedMethod()` → `gatewayMethodDescriptors` lookup
- `resolveSessionActionRegisteredScopes()` → `sessionActions` lookup

So in gateway mode **every plugin-registered gateway method / session action
fails to resolve its declared scope**, falls through to the `ADMIN_SCOPE`
default, and is then rejected for any `operator.write` client — e.g. a bridge
plugin calling its own gateway method registered with `operator.write`.

`method-scopes.ts` is the only place in the channel/gateway code still reading
`activeRegistry` directly; the rest of the codebase already uses
`getActivePluginChannelRegistryFromState()` (which returns
`channel.registry ?? activeRegistry`). The existing tests only exercised the
`setActivePluginRegistry`/`activeRegistry` path, never the channel-pinned
(gateway) path — which is why this shipped.

## Fix

Use `getActivePluginChannelRegistryFromState()` for both lookups, so scope
resolution works in **both** gateway and standalone/test modes. One-line import
swap + the two call sites.

## Tests

Adds regression tests to `method-scopes.test.ts` exercising the **channel-pinned**
path (`pinActivePluginChannelRegistry`) for both a gateway method and a session
action — the path that previously had no coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Built `main` (Node 24.16, `pnpm build`) and ran the new tests against the real
resolver + the real `pinActivePluginChannelRegistry` (not a mock):

**Without the fix** (revert `method-scopes.ts`, keep the test) — both fail:
```
× resolves a plugin gateway method scope from the channel-pinned registry
    AssertionError: expected [] to deeply equal [ 'operator.write' ]
× resolves a plugin session action scope from the channel-pinned registry
    AssertionError: expected [ 'operator.admin', …(5) ] to deeply equal [ 'operator.read' ]
```
(i.e. the plugin method resolves to "unknown" → admin-only, and the session
action falls back to the broad CLI default — exactly the production symptom.)

**With the fix** — full suite green:
```
✓ src/gateway/method-scopes.test.ts (77 tests)   # ×3 project configs = 231 passed
```

**Production:** this exact fix is deployed on our own OpenClaw gateway (source
install, v2026.5.28) running an MCP bridge plugin that registers
`operator.write` gateway methods; with the patch those calls succeed, without it
they were rejected as admin-only.

> Not run: a full network-client demo with a dedicated `operator.write`-scoped
> token (the gateway has no CLI flag to downscope a local caller; the local
> operator is always admin, which masks the bug). The unit regression drives the
> real resolver through the real channel-pin path, and the fix is in production.

## Gates
Against `main` (Node 24.16): `pnpm build` ✅, `pnpm check` (whole-repo
lint+typecheck) ✅, full `pnpm test` ✅ (incl. `method-scopes.test.ts` 77/77).

## Notes
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit (per CONTRIBUTING).
- "Allow edits by maintainers" enabled.
